APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper addresses cross-lingual POS tagging for low-resource languages, a core NLP topic clearly in scope for top NLP/ML venues. It targets the right audience by proposing a BiLSTM with an explicit noise layer and comparing against strong baselines (Duong et al. 2014a) on standard datasets (Europarl, CoNLL) and real low-resource languages (Malagasy, Kinyarwanda). The problem formulation, use of joint training on small gold data plus noisy projections, and evaluation by POS tagging accuracy follow methods and standards expected at a top conference. Positioning and related-work coverage are reported as adequate, supporting strong relevance to the venue community. | B(5): This paper addresses a core NLP problem (cross-lingual/low-resource POS tagging) using methods (BiLSTM with an explicit noise layer and joint training on gold and projected data) and evaluations (Europarl, CoNLL simulation and real Malagasy/Kinyarwanda data) that are standard and expected at top-tier NLP/ML venues. It compares to relevant baselines including prior state-of-the-art (Duong et al., 2014a) and demonstrates improved performance, indicating clear relevance to the venue's audience. Minor reporting omissions (hyperparameters not reported) do not outweigh the strong topical and methodological fit.

It compares to relevant baselines including prior state-of-the-art (Duong et al., 2014a) and demonstrates improved performance, indicating clear relevance to the venue's audience. ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper introduces a genuinely new methodological element—a sequence tagging model with an explicit noise layer that learns to correct cross-lingual projection errors—implemented as a BiLSTM jointly trained on small gold data and larger noisy projected annotations. It clearly distinguishes itself from prior work (e.g., Duong et al.) and demonstrates empirical gains across eight simulated low-resource settings and two real languages (Malagasy, Kinyarwanda), supporting that the approach is more than a simple combination of known techniques. The explicit modeling of projection noise is a non-trivial extension likely to inform researchers working on low-resource cross-lingual POS tagging. | B(4): The paper proposes a novel sequence-tagging approach by adding an explicit noise layer to a BiLSTM trained jointly on small gold data and noisy cross-lingual projected annotations, which is a clear methodological contribution beyond prior baselines (e.g., Duong et al., 2014). The problem (cross-lingual POS projection for low-resource languages) is well-studied, so the work is not a radical departure, but the explicit modeling of projection noise and joint training setup distinguish it from existing methods and yield improved results on both simulated and real low-resource languages, which would be of interest to experts.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper introduces a genuinely new methodological element—a sequence tagging model with an explicit noise layer that learns to correct cross-lingual projection errors—implemented as a BiLSTM jointly trained on small gold data and larger noisy projected annotations. It clearly distinguishes itself from prior work (e.g., Duong et al.) and demonstrates empirical gains across eight simulated low-resource settings and two real languages (Malagasy, Kinyarwanda), supporting that the approach is more than a simple combination of known techniques. The explicit modeling of projection noise is a non-trivial extension likely to inform researchers working on low-resource cross-lingual POS tagging.

The problem (cross-lingual POS projection for low-resource languages) is well-studied, so the work is not a radical departure, but the explicit modeling of projection noise and joint training setup distinguish it from existing methods and yield improved results on both simulated and real low-resource languages, which would be of interest to experts.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Experiments are substantial: evaluation on eight simulated low-resource settings (Europarl/CoNLL) plus two real low-resource languages (Malagasy, Kinyarwanda) and comparisons to a strong prior baseline (Duong et al., 2014a). Appropriate ablations/sensitivity are present via multiple BiLSTM variants (Annotated only, Projected only, Ann+Proj) which serve as implicit ablation for the noise-layer approach. Related work is described as adequate and limitations (noise in projection, need for filtering, small gold data) are discussed, but reproducibility is weakened because code is not mentioned and hyperparameters are not reported. | B(4): Experiments are fairly comprehensive: the model is evaluated against prior state-of-the-art and multiple baselines across eight simulated low-resource settings and two real low-resource languages, showing clear comparisons. Appropriate ablation-style baselines (Annotated only, Projected only, Ann+Proj) and an explicit noise layer are included to analyze the contribution of modeling projection noise. Related work coverage is reported as adequate and limitations of projection noise are discussed. However, reproducibility is weakened by missing hyperparameter reporting and no mention of released code, so there are minor gaps.

reviewer_a.justification: Experiments are substantial: evaluation on eight simulated low-resource settings (Europarl/CoNLL) plus two real low-resource languages (Malagasy, Kinyarwanda) and comparisons to a strong prior baseline (Duong et al., 2014a). Appropriate ablations/sensitivity are present via multiple BiLSTM variants (Annotated only, Projected only, Ann+Proj) which serve as implicit ablation for the noise-layer approach. Related work is described as adequate and limitations (noise in projection, need for filtering, small gold data) are discussed, but reproducibility is weakened because code is not mentioned and hyperparameters are not reported.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper tackles the important practical problem of POS tagging for low-resource languages and demonstrates a concrete method (BiLSTM trained jointly on small gold data plus noisy projected data with an explicit noise layer) that yields equal or better performance than prior state-of-the-art on eight simulated settings and improves results on two real low-resource languages (Malagasy, Kinyarwanda). By explicitly modeling projection noise and showing that naive use of projected labels is harmful, the work is likely to change how researchers harness parallel data and inspire follow-up work on noise-aware cross-lingual supervision. Its applicability to multiple languages and the clear gains over earlier baselines make it likely to be adopted and extended, giving it strong but not necessarily transformative impact. | B(4): The paper tackles the important problem of low-resource POS tagging and distant supervision by proposing a BiLSTM model with an explicit noise layer to correct cross-lingual projection errors. It shows equal or better performance than prior state-of-the-art across eight simulated languages and outperforms previous methods on two real low-resource languages (Malagasy and Kinyarwanda), which should influence how researchers treat noisy projected annotations. By demonstrating that filtering and modeling projection noise is necessary, the work opens useful directions for other low-resource and distant-supervision tasks and is likely to be adopted or extended in related sequence-tagging and cross-lingual transfer research.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles the important practical problem of POS tagging for low-resource languages and demonstrates a concrete method (BiLSTM trained jointly on small gold data plus noisy projected data with an explicit noise layer) that yields equal or better performance than prior state-of-the-art on eight simulated settings and improves results on two real low-resource languages (Malagasy, Kinyarwanda). By explicitly modeling projection noise and showing that naive use of projected labels is harmful, the work is likely to change how researchers harness parallel data and inspire follow-up work on noise-aware cross-lingual supervision. Its applicability to multiple languages and the clear gains over earlier baselines make it likely to be adopted and extended, giving it strong but not necessarily transformative impact.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The main claims are supported by experimental evidence: the proposed BiLSTM with an explicit noise layer is evaluated against Duong et al. (2014a) and multiple sensible baselines across eight simulated and two real low-resource language settings, showing equal or improved POS accuracy. There are no mathematical derivations to check, and the experimental setup uses appropriate baselines, but the summary indicates missing details that affect reproducibility and confidence (hyperparameters not reported, no mention of statistical significance testing or available code). These are minor but important gaps; they do not, however, appear to invalidate the core technical claims. | B(4): The main claims are supported by empirical evidence: the BiLSTM with an explicit noise layer is evaluated against Duong et al. (2014a) and multiple sensible baselines across eight simulated Europarl settings and two real low-resource languages (Malagasy, Kinyarwanda), showing equal or better performance. There are no reported mathematical derivations to check, but none appear necessary for the presented model. The experimental methodology is broadly sound (appropriate baselines and datasets), though the summary notes missing details such as hyperparameter reporting and statistical significance testing which are minor methodological gaps. Overall the conclusions follow from the results, with only limited missing experimental details.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(5): The paper compares against an appropriate state-of-the-art baseline (Duong et al., 2014a) and several sensible ablations (supervised maxent, BiLSTM variants including Ann, Projected, and Ann+Proj) using the same datasets (CoNLL/Europarl for simulations; Malagasy and Kinyarwanda for real low-resource tests) and the same POS accuracy metric. It explicitly analyzes projection noise and the effect of modeling/filtering it, reports cases where prior methods are matched or outperformed (equal or better on eight simulated settings; superior on two real languages), and the related work coverage is described as adequate, indicating comprehensive and fair comparisons. | B(4): The paper compares to appropriate and recent baselines including the prior state-of-the-art (Duong et al., 2014a), supervised and projection-only models, and several BiLSTM ablations, evaluated on the same datasets (Europarl, CoNLL simulations, Malagasy and Kinyarwanda) with POS accuracy. It also analyzes projection noise and includes ablations (Annotated, Projected, Ann+Proj) that clarify differences from baselines. However, some reproducibility/details that affect fairness (hyperparameters not reported, code not mentioned) are missing, preventing a full 5/5 rating despite adequate related-work coverage.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against an appropriate state-of-the-art baseline (Duong et al., 2014a) and several sensible ablations (supervised maxent, BiLSTM variants including Ann, Projected, and Ann+Proj) using the same datasets (CoNLL/Europarl for simulations; Malagasy and Kinyarwanda for real low-resource tests) and the same POS accuracy metric. It explicitly analyzes projection noise and the effect of modeling/filtering it, reports cases where prior methods are matched or outperformed (equal or better on eight simulated settings; superior on two real languages), and the related work coverage is described as adequate, indicating comprehensive and fair comparisons.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized with a clear flow from problem motivation to method and experiments, and the main contributions are stated early (explicit noise layer, BiLSTM joint training on gold and projected data, empirical gains on simulated and real low-resource languages). Notation and related work appear consistent and the writing quality is described as good, making the narrative easy to follow. A minor presentation issue is that experimental details important for reproducibility (e.g., hyperparameters) are not reported and the summary does not indicate whether figures/tables fully convey results, preventing a perfect score. | B(4): The paper is well-organized with a clear flow from motivation through the BiLSTM method to experiments, and the main contributions (noise layer, joint training with gold and projected data) are stated early. Notation and explanations appear consistent and readable (writing quality labeled as good), and experimental presentation is informative given the comprehensive set of datasets and baselines compared. A minor presentation shortcoming is that hyperparameters are not reported and code availability is not mentioned, which detracts slightly from clarity and reproducibility of the experiments.

REPLICABILITY.justification: [Consensus avg=2] A(3): Important implementation details are missing (hyperparameters and training specifics are not reported), and the data preprocessing pipeline is not clearly described, which limits exact reproduction. The datasets used are standard/public (Europarl, CoNLL) or cited (Malagasy and Kinyarwanda from Garrette & Baldridge), so data access is feasible. The model architecture (BiLSTM with an explicit noise layer) and the evaluation metric (POS tagging accuracy) are described, but code is not provided, so reimplementation would require nontrivial guesswork. Overall this supports partial reproducibility. | B(2): Critical training details are missing: hyperparameters and model configuration are not reported (Hyperparameters reported: False) and code availability is not mentioned, making exact reimplementation difficult. The data sources are listed (Europarl, CoNLL, Malagasy, Kinyarwanda) and the main metric (POS tagging accuracy) is stated, but the preprocessing pipeline and precise training/evaluation procedures are not described. Given these gaps, reproduction would be difficult without substantial guesswork.

main_claims: Introduces a novel sequence tagging approach that learns to correct errors from cross-lingual projected POS tags using an explicit noise layer.
Proposes a BiLSTM-based model trained jointly on a small gold-annotated corpus and noisy projected annotations to improve POS tagging for low-resource languages.

Demonstrates equal or better performance than prior state-of-the-art on eight simulated low-resource settings and outperforms previous methods on two real low-resource languages (Malagasy and Kinyarwanda).
Shows that directly projected labels are very noisy and that filtering and modeling projection noise is necessary for effective use of parallel data.
_summary.methodology: Train a bidirectional LSTM POS tagger jointly on a small gold-annotated dataset and larger noisy cross-lingually projected data, with an added explicit noise layer to model and correct projection errors.
datasets: CoNLL data (Buchholz and Marsi, 2006) for annotated data in simulation experiments
baselines: Two-output maximum entropy model (Duong et al.)
Supervised maximum entropy model trained on annotated data
BiLSTM Annotated (trained on annotated data only)
BiLSTM Projected (trained on projected data only)
BiLSTM Ann+Proj (trained on union of annotated and projected data)
limitations: Directly projected labels are very noisy and unwise to use without filtering or modeling.
Cross-lingual projection suffers from bias and noise due to poor word alignments and cross-lingual syntactic divergence.
Gold annotated data is expensive and only small annotated datasets are assumed available.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - POS tagging (cross-lingual projection for low-resource languages)
